attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with three days of worsening back pain and difficulty walking. She gave birth five days ago via normal vaginal delivery with epidural anaesthesia. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications other than paracetamol. She also reports fever and rigors over the past 24 hours.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. There is marked tenderness over the lumbar spine. Neurological examination reveals reduced power (3/5) in both lower limbs with hyperreflexia and upgoing plantar responses. Sensation is reduced below T10.

Blood tests show white cell count 18 × 10⁹/L (4-11), C-reactive protein 220 mg/L (<5).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic triad of back pain, fever, and neurological deficits, strongly suggesting spinal epidural abscess. The recent epidural procedure during childbirth is a significant risk factor. The progression from back pain to neurological deficits over days is typical, and urgent imaging and surgical intervention are required to prevent permanent neurological damage.

Spinal epidural abscess typically presents with severe localised back pain and tenderness, followed by radicular symptoms and progressive neurological deficits. The presence of fever and elevated inflammatory markers supports an infectious process. The recent epidural procedure provides a clear mechanism for infection.

Guillain-Barré syndrome typically presents with ascending paralysis starting in the feet and moving upward. While it can occur post-partum, the presence of fever, localised back pain, and upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia and upgoing plantars) make this diagnosis unlikely.

Multiple sclerosis can present with various neurological symptoms, but the acute onset, fever, and recent epidural are not typical. Multiple sclerosis rarely presents for the first time in the immediate post-partum period with such rapid progression.

Sciatica is common in pregnancy and post-partum but would not cause fever, bilateral neurological deficits, or elevated inflammatory markers. It typically presents with unilateral radiating leg pain and normal reflexes.

Transverse myelitis can cause similar neurological symptoms but typically does not present with fever or such marked inflammatory markers. The temporal relationship to the epidural procedure makes an infectious cause more likely.

Management of spinal epidural abscess requires urgent magnetic resonance imaging to confirm the diagnosis and determine the extent of compression, followed by surgical decompression and appropriate antibiotic therapy. Common causative organisms include Staphylococcus aureus and streptococci.
learningPoints: Spinal epidural abscess is a neurosurgical emergency requiring prompt recognition and treatment to prevent permanent neurological deficit.
Risk factors for epidural abscess include recent epidural procedures, immunosuppression, and intravenous drug use.
The classic triad of back pain, fever, and neurological deficits is present in only a minority of cases.
references: Akhondi H, Baker MB. Epidural Abscess. [Updated 2023 May 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK535450/

D'Angelo R, Smiley RM, Riley ET, Segal S. Serious complications related to obstetric anesthesia: the serious complication repository project of the Society for Obstetric Anesthesia and Perinatology. Anesthesiology. Jun 2014;120(6):1505-12. doi:10.1097/ALN.0000000000000253

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
